name: Boltaña, Aínsa and Mediano Reservoir Route
name_original: Boltaña, Aínsa y Embalse del Mediano
description: Enjoy an easy 18.8-mile road cycling route from Boltaña to Aínsa, featuring reservoir views, river scenery, and historic landmarks.
region_intro: Road cycling around Boltaña, situated in the Huesca province of Aragón, Spain, offers diverse terrain for cyclists. The region is characterized by its location within the Aragonese Pyrenees, featuring significant elevation changes, river valleys like the Ara River, and large reservoirs such as Embalse del Mediano. Cyclists can expect well-paved surfaces on many routes, with some segments presenting challenging ascents and descents. The landscape includes views of canyons, mountains, and natural parks, providing varied backdrops for road cycling routes. Best road cycling routes around Boltaña The most popular road cycling route is Boltaña, Aínsa and Mediano Reservoir Route, an 18.8 miles (30.3 km) easy trail that takes 1 hour 22 minutes to complete. This route offers scenic views of the reservoir and river. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Port of Serrablo and Nocito from Boltaña, a difficult 65.6 miles (105.6 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and provides views of the Sierra and Guara canyons, and Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park. Local road cyclists also love the Puente de los Moscarales – Mesón de Fuebla loop from Aínsa, a 45.6 miles (73.4 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain with challenging climbs, often completed in about 3 hours 55 minutes. Road cycling around Boltaña is defined by the Pyrenees Mountains, the Ara River valley, and dramatic canyons. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easier rides to demanding ascents. The routes in Boltaña are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 2,200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Boltaña's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Boltaña?
A: Boltaña offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 60 different tours to explore. These routes cater to various fitness levels, from easy rides to very challenging ascents.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Boltaña?
A: Yes, Boltaña provides several easy road cycling routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. An excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e923673706/boltana-ainsa-and-mediano-reservoir-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boltaña, Aínsa and Mediano Reservoir Route&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 30.3 km tour that offers scenic views of the reservoir and river with moderate elevation gain.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Boltaña?
A: For experienced cyclists looking for a challenge, Boltaña offers numerous demanding routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926866234/port-of-serrablo-and-nocito-from-boltana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Serrablo and Nocito from Boltaña&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 105.6 km path with significant elevation gain, providing spectacular views of the Sierra and Guara canyons, and Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/993081" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de los Moscarales – Mesón de Fuebla loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt;, a 73.4 km route through mountainous terrain.

Q: What kind of landscapes and natural features can I expect to see while road cycling around Boltaña?
A: Road cycling around Boltaña immerses you in diverse and stunning landscapes. You'll encounter breathtaking views of the Pyrenees Mountains, ride alongside the wild Ara River, and pass by the expansive Embalse del Mediano reservoir. The routes also offer glimpses of dramatic canyons, such as those within the Sierra y Cañones de Guara Natural Park, and the majestic scenery leading towards Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes pass by or offer views of significant landmarks. You can cycle past the historic Boltaña Castle, a 10th-century ruin offering a glimpse into the region's past. The nearby Aínsa Old Town is also a historical site with ancient buildings. For natural attractions, you might encounter highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6125681" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aso River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the picturesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1390201" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellós River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Boltaña?
A: The best season for road cycling in Boltaña is generally spring and autumn when the weather is milder and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can also be suitable, especially in the mornings or late afternoons, though some routes might be more exposed to heat. Winter cycling is possible but requires careful planning due to potentially colder temperatures and snow at higher elevations in the Pyrenees.

Q: Are there any loop routes for road cycling around Boltaña?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Boltaña are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/993081" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de los Moscarales – Mesón de Fuebla loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5329785" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aínsa Old Town – Church of Banastón loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt;, which provide circular journeys through the region's diverse terrain.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Boltaña?
A: The road cycling routes in Boltaña are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the diverse terrain, from river valleys to challenging mountain passes, and the stunning views of the Pyrenees, canyons, and natural parks. The well-paved surfaces on many routes and the variety of options for different ability levels are also highly appreciated.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: While specific cafes are not listed for every route, the region around Boltaña and nearby towns like Aínsa offer various amenities. You'll find opportunities for refreshments and accommodation in these villages, which are often incorporated into or are accessible from the cycling routes. It's always a good idea to check local services before heading out.

Q: Are there routes that offer views of the Pyrenees?
A: Absolutely. Boltaña is situated within the Aragonese Pyrenees, so many road cycling routes offer spectacular views of these majestic mountains. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926866234/port-of-serrablo-and-nocito-from-boltana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Serrablo and Nocito from Boltaña&lt;/a&gt; provide expansive vistas of the Pyrenean peaks and surrounding natural parks.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on road cycling routes in Boltaña?
A: The elevation gain on road cycling routes in Boltaña varies significantly depending on the route's difficulty. Easier routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e923673706/boltana-ainsa-and-mediano-reservoir-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boltaña, Aínsa and Mediano Reservoir Route&lt;/a&gt;, have moderate gains of around 270 meters. More challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926866234/port-of-serrablo-and-nocito-from-boltana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Serrablo and Nocito from Boltaña&lt;/a&gt;, can feature over 2,100 meters of ascent, making them suitable for very fit cyclists.
tips: Viewpoint to the Mediano Reservoir in a restored building where the cereals were winnowed (separating grain and straw with the wind) after threshing.

Small circular temple from the year 1665 that shelters the cross that commemorates the reconquest of Aínsa by the Christian warriors. Within the popular narrative of Muslim exile, this cross has its own legend.
At the level of the bridge, the Ara river forms beautiful natural pools. It is tempting to go swimming there.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like for this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is generally classified as an easy road ride, suitable for various fitness levels. However, it's important to note that while much of the route is paved, some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride, which is a crucial consideration for road cyclists. Be prepared for varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What scenic views and landmarks can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll enjoy diverse scenery, including views of the Cinca River and the impressive Peña Montañesa. The route passes by the historic castle of Aínsa. You can also take a break at the Ara River Natural Pool at Boltaña and enjoy the expansive views from the Mediano Reservoir Viewpoint (Mirador del Aventadero). Don't miss the Cruz Cubierta Monument near Aínsa.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns of Boltaña or Aínsa, which serve as excellent starting points for this route. Both towns are well-equipped to accommodate visitors.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 30.3 kilometers (18.8 miles) long and typically takes around 1 hour and 23 minutes to complete by bike, depending on your pace and how many stops you make to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions outside of the peak summer heat and winter cold. Spring and autumn are often ideal, providing comfortable temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a road cycling path, the towns of Boltaña and Aínsa, and the natural areas around them, are generally welcoming to dogs. If you plan to bring your dog, ensure they are comfortable with cycling alongside or in a trailer, and always keep them on a leash, especially in populated areas or near wildlife.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Sobrarbe region?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public routes like this one in the Sobrarbe region. However, always respect private property and any local regulations you might encounter, especially if you venture off the main path.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other trails. You'll find sections that overlap with official paths such as "ZZ005 - Peñas Cinglas y Coda Sartén," "Senderos del Sobrarbe," and "ZZ006 - Trilogía de San Vicente v2.0," among others. This offers opportunities to explore further if you wish.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families?
faqs.answer: Given its 'easy' classification and moderate length, this route can be suitable for families with children who are comfortable with road cycling. However, be mindful of the potential unpaved sections mentioned earlier, and ensure all riders are equipped for varied terrain and traffic conditions.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route for cyclists?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Boltaña and Aínsa are well-established towns with amenities. You'll find various cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options that cater to visitors, including cyclists. Many establishments are accustomed to welcoming those exploring the region by bike.

faqs.question: Is there water available along the route?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific water refill stations highlighted directly on the route, you will pass through or near the towns of Boltaña and Aínsa, where you can easily find shops or cafes to purchase water and other supplies. It's always recommended to carry sufficient water for your ride.
